disclosure: Specific response-rate statistic removed. No single verifiable public figure was cited, so the claim was rewritten as a directional industry pattern.

claims.text: Most service businesses take hours, not minutes, to respond to a new lead
Fast response converts meaningfully better than slow response, making follow-up speed a higher-leverage fix than added ad spend in that quadrant
The 2x2 lead-volume/response-speed framework as a way to diagnose whether the bottleneck is acquisition or follow-up

disclosure: The 7x/60x figures come directly from the Harvard Business Review study 'The Short Life of Online Sales Leads' (Oldroyd, McElheran, Elkington, 2011, n=2,241 US firms). The one-hour response rate benchmark is a generalized industry pattern rather than a single verified statistic, presented as a qualified estimate rather than an exact figure.

claims.text: Harvard Business Review published a study auditing 2,241 US companies that submitted test web leads and timed the response of each
Firms that made contact with a lead within an hour were nearly seven times more likely to qualify it than firms that waited just one hour longer
Firms that waited a full day to respond were more than sixty times less likely to qualify the same lead compared to fast responders
Industry benchmarking puts the one-hour lead response rate for service businesses below one in five
Black Arrow builds automated response systems that engage every new lead within minutes
